coredata.dc:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with efficient red emission from conjugated perylene for multiple applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Cuixia
Zhang, Sainan
Liu, Mengdi
He, Junhui
coredata.dc:description: Despite its significance for applications related to plant growth, the development of carbon dots (CDs) with bright red emissions still faces multiple obstacles. Herein, red emission hydrophobic carbon dots (RH-CDs) featuring one emission peak centered at 594 nm and a shoulder emission peak at 630 nm with a quantum yield of 34 % and a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 100 nm (580 nm–680 nm) were synthesized by adopting large conjugated perylene anhydride and aromatic amine precursors under solvothermal conditions. Density functional theory calculations support the method of using larger conjugated systems and higher graphite N doping to increase red emissions from CDs. With a post-surface functionalization strategy, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-modified RH-CDs (QY = 12 %) were utilized as an anti-counterfeiting ink for information printing. Moreover, both blue-red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and light conversion poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)-CDs film were fabricated with emission spectra that are consistent with the absorption spectra of plants, suggesting that the prepared RH-CDs may have widespread application potential as a stable fluorescent material in plant growth.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Anti-counterfeiting
Plant growth LED
Light conversion films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of CDs. a) FT-IR spectra and b) PL emission spectra of CDs synthesized with varied concentrations of AcOH; c) PL emission spectra of CDs synthesized with varied reaction times; d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of RH-CDs in EtOH solution; e) Excitation-emission mapping and f) PL decay curve of RH-CDs in DCM solution.

Characterization of CDs. TEM, HRTEM (inset) images, and size distribution histograms of a) RH-CDs-6 and b) RH-CDs-10; c) Raman, d) XRD, e) FT-IR spectra, and f) XPS survey spectra of RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10.
High-resolution a,d) C 1s, b,e) N 1s, and c,f) O 1s XPS spectra of a,b,c) RH-CDs-6 and d,e,f) RH-CDs-10, respectively.

a) DFT calculations of four molecular models with increasing condensation from left to right. Molecular structures and calculated LUMOs and HOMOs of the four models: PTCDA, PTCDA-o-PDA, o-PDA-PTCDA-DAP, and PTCDA-DAP. b) A scheme illustrating possible formation processes for the CDs.

Digital images of numeric encryption systems under a, c) natural light and b,d) UV light. The scale bars are 1 cm. e) PL emission and excitation spectra of PVP-CDs; f) Digital photo (inset) and electro-luminance spectrum of the working LED; g) The CIE color coordinates and CCT; h) PL emission and excitation spectra of PMMA-CDs film and digital photos of films with (right) and without (left) CDs under UV light (inset). i) Transmission spectra of blank PMMA and PMMA-CDs and digital photos under ambient light, respectively (inset). j) Outdoor solar spectra measured below the PMMA and PMMA-CDs films.
a) PL spectra and b) normalized PL intensity of PVP-CDs after treatment at varied temperatures; c) Photostability of CDs in DCM solution and d) PMMA-CDs film under UV radiation (365 nm).
Schematic illustration of RH-CDs synthesis and applications.

head.ce:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with efficient red emission from conjugated perylene for multiple applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite its significance for applications related to plant growth, the development of carbon dots (CDs) with bright red emissions still faces multiple obstacles. Herein, red emission hydrophobic carbon dots (RH-CDs) featuring one emission peak centered at 594 nm and a shoulder emission peak at 630 nm with a quantum yield of 34 % and a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 100 nm (580 nm–680 nm) were synthesized by adopting large conjugated perylene anhydride and aromatic amine precursors under solvothermal conditions. Density functional theory calculations support the method of using larger conjugated systems and higher graphite N doping to increase red emissions from CDs. With a post-surface functionalization strategy, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-modified RH-CDs (QY = 12 %) were utilized as an anti-counterfeiting ink for information printing. Moreover, both blue-red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and light conversion poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)-CDs film were fabricated with emission spectra that are consistent with the absorption spectra of plants, suggesting that the prepared RH-CDs may have widespread application potential as a stable fluorescent material in plant growth.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Red emission hydrophobic carbon dots (RH-CDs) for multiple applications were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn extensive attention since they were first reported by Xu et al. [1] due to their unique optical properties [2], excellent biocompatibility [3], and low toxicity [4,5]. To date, CDs have been widely used in many fields, including bioimaging [6–10], optoelectronic devices [11–14], sensing [15], and so on. Particularly, red-emissive CDs (red CDs) may have numerous potentials in agricultural applications because UV-to-red light conversion is important to plants. On one hand, high-energy UV light between 200 nm and 400 nm not only inhibits photosynthesis but also makes plants more susceptible to diseases. On the other hand, red light in 600–700 nm can effectively improve plant growth since photosynthetic pigments absorb light in the red region [16,17]. Thus, light conversion films and plant growth light-emitting diodes (LEDs) doped with red fluorescent materials can effectively promote plant growth and increase crop yield [18,19]. Nevertheless, CDs generally exhibit dominant emissions in the blue region [15], which restricts their widespread application in the agriculture field. Therefore, CDs with significant red emission should be synthesized using rational design and intensively investigated to broaden their applications.

As a low-cost organic dye, 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) contains five conjugated aromatic rings and two anhydride groups that are easily reactive. The amino group of the aromatic molecule o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) could react with anhydride, promoting the creation of lager conjugated structures in CDs as well as nitrogen atom doping. Herein, as was expected, novel hydrophobic red-emission CDs (named as RH-CDs) were designed and synthesized by the solvothermal method using PTCDA and o-PDA as precursors, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent, and acetic acid as catalyst (Scheme 1 ). The obtained RH-CDs demonstrated one emission peak centered at 594 nm and a shoulder emission peak at 630 nm with a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 100 nm (580 nm–680 nm) and a quantum yield of 34 %. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that larger conjugated structures (particularly those containing graphitic nitrogen) in CDs improve their longer wavelength emission. Importantly, the hydrophobicity of the RH-CDs allowed for the production of a homogeneous, extremely transparent PMMA-CDs film with green-to-red spectral conversion. With a QY of 23 %, the as-prepared PMMA-CDs film exhibits a 1.4 % increase in red radiation compared to pure PMMA film. Additionally, the plant growth LED lamp was also obtained by combining PVP-modified RH-CDs with a blue LED chip. The emission spectrum of LED in blue and red regions exactly matches the absorption spectrum of plant photosynthesis, demonstrating enormous potential for application in agriculture.

3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), polyvinylpyrrolidone K-30 (PVP), and poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co. o-Phenylenediamine (o-PDA) and ultra-dry N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9 %) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Science & Technology Co. Glacial acetic acid (99.5 %, AR) and dichloromethane were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. All reagents were used directly without further treatment.

0.1 g PTCDA and 0.92 g o-PDA were dissolved in 20 mL DMF, and an appropriate amount of acetic acid (0.5 % v/v corresponding to 100 μL acetic acid) was added. The reaction mixture was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor and heated to 220 °C for 6–16 h. After the reaction, a dark brown-red liquid was obtained. The mixture was centrifuged and repeatedly extracted by the dichloromethane/water system until there was no flocculant in the solution. The dichloromethane solution phase was dialyzed for 48 h (the molecular weight cut-off of the dialysis membrane was 1000 Da), and pure water was replaced every 30 min for the first 6 h. Finally, CD was left in the dialysis bag and freeze-dried to obtain a solid powder.

0.2 g PVP powder was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. 1 mg CDs powder was added and stirred at 50 °C for 30 min, then the solvent was evaporated. The obtained powder was transferred to a vacuum drying oven, dried at 60 °C for 3 h, and the solid was ground to obtain PVP-CDs.

The CDs-PVP was dispersed in epoxy resin, mixed evenly, then directly coated on a commercial blue light chip (430 nm), and dried naturally at room temperature. The emission performance was tested in a dark room.

0.4 mg of CDs and 0.1 g of PMMA were dissolved in 5 mL of chloroform, and stirred for 4 h at room temperature. Then we transferred 1 mL of the mixed solution and dropped it onto a 7 cm × 2.5 cm glass plate. After drying at room temperature, the PMMA film was obtained. The preparation method for blank PMMA film was similar, but without CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were performed (JEM-2100F, JEOL) at an operating voltage of 200 kV and used to characterize the particle size and crystal lattice of the CDs. Raman spectra were obtained using an inVia™-Qontor confocal Raman microscope (Renishaw) under laser excitation at a wavelength of 365 nm, which was used to determine the intrinsic structure of the CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Al Kα monochromatized source (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) to further examine the chemical composition and functional groups of the CDs. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the RH-CDs were recorded from 4000 cm−1–400 cm−1 using KBr pellets on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by Agilent Cary 5000. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a FLS-1000 luminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh) at room temperature in dichloromethane solution.

The B3LYP density functional method with D3 dispersion correction was adopted in this work to perform all computations. The 6-31G(d) basis set was employed for the atoms in the geometry optimization. The Spartan 16 suite of programs was used throughout this study.

The hydrophobic red emission CDs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using 0.1 g PTCDA and 0.92 g o-PDA dissolved in 20 mL of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) with acetic acid (AcOH) at 220 °C for varied reaction times. Acetic acid was selected as a catalyst to promote the condensation reaction between anhydride and amino. As shown in their FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 a), the intensity of N–CO (1664 cm−1) peak and C–N–C (1377 cm−1) peak increases with increasing acetic acid usage in the reaction, whereas the intensity of N–H (3366 cm−1) gradually decreases. This result verified that increasing the amount of acetic acid is favorable to the imine condensation reaction between PTCDA and o-PDA.

Furthermore, we found that, in the absence of acid, the obtained CDs possess a main emission peak at 580 nm and a shoulder peak at 546 nm (Fig. 1b). Among them, the photoluminescence (PL) peak at 546 nm is attributed to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), a dimer obtained from the self-condensation of o-PDA molecules [28]. With the addition of acetic acid, the main emission peak red-shifts to 594 nm, the shoulder peak at 546 nm disappears, and a new shoulder peak appears at 630 nm, suggesting that the condensation between PTCDA and o-PDA is favorable for the generation of red-emission CDs. It is noted form Fig. 1b that the intensity of the emission peak at 630 nm exhibits a tendency of first increasing and then dropping when the amount of acetic acid increases from 0.25 % to 5 % (volume fraction). And the highest fluorescence intensity at 630 nm could be obtained by adding 0.5 % acetic acid. Similarly, photographs of CDs generated with varied acid dosages (dispersed in dichloromethane (DCM) solution) under a 365 nm lamp revealed that the CDs emitted the brightest red emission when the acid dosage was 0.5 % (Fig. S2). Furthermore, it could be found that nitric acid has no reaction-promoting effect because the fluorescence performance of CDs generated by replacing acetic acid with nitric acid (Fig. S3) is comparable to that of CDs obtained without adding acid (Fig. 1b). These results imply that acetic acid is not only a catalyst for speeding reaction rates, but it also influences the carbon nucleus and surface state of CDs by regulating polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization reactions, consequently modulating their optical characteristics [29,30].

Additionally, the effects of the reaction solvent, precursor ratio, reaction temperature, and duration on the optical characteristics of CDs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the CDs synthesized using ethanol as the reaction solvent emit yellow light with a peak at 590 nm, whereas those obtained with water as the solvent emit green light with a peak at 510 nm. And DMF as the solvent is conducive to the red emission of the CDs. It is hypothesized that the use of DMF in the synthesis of CDs enhances nitrogen doping in the CDs structure. The optical properties of CDs prepared under varied molar ratios of PTCDA to o-PDA and different reaction temperatures are shown in Figure S5a and Figure S5b, respectively. These findings suggest that an optimal precursor ratio (PTCDA:o-PDA = 1:33) is one of the most important factors in obtaining efficient red-emitting CDs, and the optimal reaction temperature is 220 °C. Furthermore, reaction time plays an important role in the performance of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the peak intensity of CDs at 630 nm increases significantly as the reaction time extends from 6 h to 10 h, but slightly decreases when the reaction time further extends to 16 h. Thus, the optimal red CDs were synthesized by reaction at 220 °C for 10 h in a DMF solvent containing 0.5 % acetic acid.

After extraction and dialysis purification, the CDs produced under optimal conditions exhibit one emission peak centered at 594 nm and a shoulder emission peak at 630 nm, with a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 100 nm (580–680 nm) and an absolute QY of 34 % (Fig. 1e and Table S1). Moreover, 2D contour FL spectra (Fig. 1e) demonstrate that the red CDs could be excited with excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 520 nm, and the emissions of the CDs are excitation-independent, which are consistent with their corresponding exciton absorption wavelengths. As shown in the UV–Vis absorption spectra, the CDs display an absorption band at 250–300 nm assigned to the π–π* transitions, a peak at 350 nm corresponding to the n–π* transition, and strong excitonic absorption peaks at 420–550 nm, indicating the formation of large conjugated structures in CDs [31,32]. The PL decay curve was obtained and could be fitted by a single-exponential function with a lifetime of 6.17 ns, which means that the CDs have a single emission center or are controlled by the sole energy level.

The solvent-dependent optical properties of the red CDs were also investigated. Surprisingly, the CDs could be homogeneously dispersed in organic solvents such as ethanol, methanol, and DCM, but could not be dispersed in an aqueous solution. Moreover, fluorescence quenching occurs due to the aggregation of CDs in water, demonstrating that the obtained CDs are hydrophobic. In addition, the corresponding excitation spectra of CDs in various solvents (including toluene, ethyl acetate (EA), chloroform (TCM), DCM, acetone, DMF, dimethyl sulfate (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), and methanol (MeOH)) display no significant difference (Fig. S6a). However, there are considerable changes in the PL emission spectra of CDs in solvents with varying polarities, and the higher the solvent polarity, the longer the emission wavelength of CDs (Figure S6b and Figure S7). The solvent-dependent emission properties of CDs might be related to the changes in their surface electronic states caused by the solvent dipole moment [33], which are more influenced as the solvent polarity increases. For instance, the CDs’ emission center red-shifts to 602 nm in a high-polarity methanol solution from 580 nm in a low-polarity toluene solution. These results suggest that the surface functional groups of CDs might tend to form hydrogen bonds with molecules of polar solvents [34], leading to a redshift in the fluorescence spectrum. Indeed the 1H NMR data (Fig. S8) confirmed the existence of hydrogen bonds between RH-CDs and polar solvent molecules. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations are shown in Fig. S9.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to characterize the morphology, structure, and chemical composition of as-synthesized RH-CDs. As shown in TEM images (Fig. 2 a and b), the prepared CDs are spherical particles with good dispersibility. It is worth noting that the average particle size (sp2 domain) expands from 1.4 nm to 2.2 nm as the reaction time increases from 6 h to 10 h, suggesting that a larger quantum size is conducive to the emission of longer wavelengths [6,11]. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2a and b insets) show that both the CDs obtained with 6 h (named as RH-CDs-6) and 10 h (named as RH-CDs-10) have distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite carbon [35]. In the Raman spectra of RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10 (Fig. 2c), a sharp peak around 1599 cm−1 is observed, which can be ascribed to the in-plane vibration (G band) of the graphite sp2 carbon atoms [36]. In addition, no significant vibration of disordered sp3 carbon atoms (D band) appears around 1380 cm−1, indicating the formation of ordered graphene-like carbon cores in both CDs [26].

The XRD patterns of RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10 are shown in Fig. 2d, and it could be observed that all CDs have a broad diffraction peak at about 24°, which may originate from the (002) layer spacing of the graphite plane (Fig. 2d). The surface functional groups and chemical structures of CDs were confirmed by FT-IR and XPS analyses. Compared with the precursor PTCDA, the FT-IR spectra of RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10 display new peaks attributed to the stretching of N–CO (1664 cm−1) and C–N–C (1377 cm−1), respectively, whereas the peak related to the stretching vibration of CO (1775 cm−1) in PTCDA has disappeared [10], indicating an amide condensation reaction between PTCDA and o-PDA (Fig. 2e). And the higher peak intensity of N–CO and C–N–C in RH-CDs-10 than in RH-CDs-6 suggests that extending the reaction time has enhanced the degree of reaction. Additionally, the RH-CDs exhibit strong IR absorption bands between 2800 and 3000 cm−1, which could be assigned as sp3 C–H of abundant alkyl chains [37,38]. We speculate that this is one of the reasons for the hydrophobicity of the prepared CDs. The XPS data of RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10 show similar elemental composites with C 1s (284.9 eV), N 1s (399.3 eV), and O 1s (532.2 eV) (Fig. 2f) [39]. Compared to the precursor PTCDA, which has a 4:1 ratio of C to O, the proportion of C in CDs has increased to 8:1, possibly due to the dehydration and carbonization processes that occurred throughout the solvothermal synthesis. The proportion of N is 3–4%, indicating the presence of N in CDs.

In order to further analyze the components and chemical structures of the CDs, high-resolution XPS spectra were fitted to the relevant elements. As shown in Fig. 3 a and d, the C1s spectra of both RH-CDs-6 and RH-CDs-10 could be deconvoluted into five peaks: 288.8 eV, 286.4 eV, 285.0 eV, 284.5 eV, and 283.6 eV, which belong to CO in CO–O or CO–N, C–O, C–N, sp3/sp2 carbon (C–C/CC), and C–H, respectively [40]. Compared with RH-CDs-6, the contents of CO and C–O in RH-CDs-10 increase from 6.28 % to 9.08 %–8.89 % and 11.51 %, respectively, suggesting a larger conjugated degree. As can be seen from Fig. 3b and e, the N 1s binding energy of both CDs could be deconvoluted into four peaks: graphite nitrogen (400.5 eV), tertiary nitrogen (399.7 eV), amino nitrogen (398.8 eV), and pyridine nitrogen (398.0 eV) [41]. As the reaction time increases from 6 h to 10 h, the contents of tertiary N and pyridine N increase separately from 27.86 % to 21.38 %–34.20 % and 24.51 %, while the amino N decreases from 27.38 % to 19.88 %, illustrating the further progress of the imine condensation reaction. The high content of graphite N and tertiary N in RH-CDs-10 might be one of the reasons for the red emission of CDs [42,43]. Additionally, both the O 1s spectra in Fig. 3c and f exhibit three peaks ascribed to OC–O (533.4 eV), C–O (532.0 eV), and CO (530.8 eV) [44], in which the content of OC–O is up to 34.08 % in RH-CDs-10, indicating that more carbon atoms has been oxidized into carboxyl groups. Previous studies have demonstrated that the inclusion of carboxyl groups might result in a more complicated surface state and broader FWHMs [15]. According to the above results of TEM, FT-IR, and XPS measurement, the improvement in red emission of RH-CDs-10 could be attributed to the larger conjugated system, higher graphitization degree, and higher graphite N doping in comparison with RH-CDs-6.

To elucidate the luminescence mechanism of the RH-CDs, four molecular structures: PTCDA, o-PDA-reacted PTCDA (PTCDA-o-PDA), dimer (DAP) and o-PDA-reacted PTCDA (o-PDA-PTCDA-DAP), and double DAP-reacted PTCDA (PTCDA-DAP) were simulated using DFT calculations (Fig. 4 ). The hybrid exchange-correlation density functional (B3LYP) and 6-31G (d) basis sets were used in the DFT calculations to optimize the geometric model and calculate the bandgaps of the four molecular configurations. Fig. 4 displays the geometrically optimized structures for each of the four molecular structures in vacuum, along with their distributions of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO). The bandgaps of the four molecular structures were calculated to be 2.56, 2.14, 1.97, and 1.91 eV, respectively. According to these results, we could learn that the reaction with o-PDA will significantly reduce the bandgap of PTCDA. Specifically, the calculated bandgap of PTCDA-o-PDA is 2.14 eV, corresponding to a wavelength of 579 nm, which is consistent with the main emission peak of RH-CDs at about 590 nm measured in DCM solution. Moreover, due to the self-condensation of o-PDA to form the dimer (DAP), the o-PDA-PTCDA-DAP is adapted. And the bandgap of 1.97 eV, which corresponds to PL emission at 629 nm, is in excellent agreement with the red emission of RH-CDs at 630 nm in DCM solution.

Based on the above results, a possible formation process from PTCDA and o-PDA to red-emissive CDs is proposed in Fig. 4b. Under solvothermal conditions, o-PDA and dimer (DAP) react with PTCDA, mainly forming PTCDA-o-PDA and o-PDA- PTCDA-DAP. Following orderly stacking, they condense to create huge fragments, then carbonize to form stiff graphene sheets and eventually produce red emission CDs (Fig. 4b).

To boost the application of RH-CDs, efficient solid-state red emissions are required. An effective method to avoid the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [45,46] of CDs is to disperse the CDs into a proper matrix [47,48]. Here, we produced red emission powder PVP-CDs with a QY of 12 % by dispersing the synthesized RH-CDs in PVP. The PVP-CDs could be utilized as an anti-counterfeiting ink for information printing and anti-counterfeiting due to their solid-state fluorescence emission and high fluorescence intensity [49]. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the Arabic numeral “888” was designed to be “123,” which was printed by PVP-CDs ink. The number “888” was observed under natural light, while the encrypted number “123” was immediately apparent when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp. The encryption of information in letters is shown in Fig. 5c and d. Similarly, all the letters of “That is unpleasant at the moment” were visible in sunlight, but “That is pleasant teen” appeared under UV irradiation. Therefore, with the advantages of the encryption mode and low cost, the PVP-CDs possess potential applications in the field of commercial anti-counterfeiting inks.

Additionally, the PL excitation and emission spectra of PVP-CDs are shown in Fig. 5e. Compared with RH-CDs solution, the maximum PL emission wavelength of PVP-CDs is red-shifted to 650 nm, which well matches with the absorption spectrum of plant photosynthetic pigments [50]. Blue-red LED plant grow lights, whose emission spectra in both blue and red regions are consistent with the absorption spectra of plants, were fabricated by coating the PVP-CDs with epoxy resin onto a commercial blue (430 nm) LED chip (Fig. 5f). As shown in Fig. 5g, the CIE (chromaticity coordinate) of the as-fabricated LED was (0.302,0.116), the related color temperature (CCT) was 3408 K, and the color rendering index (CRI) was 16. These results demonstrate that the prepared red-blue LED has potential applications in the supplemental illumination of indoor plants.

Moreover, to further expand the application of as-synthesized RH-CDs in plant growth, we developed a red emission light conversion film with a QY of 23 % by dispersing CDs in PMMA matrix (named as PMMA-CDs). Owing to the passivation of CDs surface by polymer and the changes in CDs surface state, the main PL emission peak of PMMA-CDs has a blue-shift of 10 nm, but the FWHM increases by 150 nm from 550 nm to 700 nm. As shown in Fig. 5h, the PMMA-CDs exhibits significant green light (490–560 nm) absorption and red light (600–700 nm) emission that could cover the absorbance of plant pigments. Fig. 5i demonstrates that, with the exception of absorption in the green region, the PMMA-CDs film maintains a transmittance comparable to that of blank PMMA film throughout the other wavelength ranges. In addition, the actual light conversion capabilities of PMMA-CDs film under outdoor sunlight were also evaluated in the autumn, Beijing, China. The photosynthetic photon flux density (PPFD) obtained under the PMMA-CDs film shows that the photosynthetic radiation of PMMA-CDs increases by 1.4 % in the red region and decreases by 5.4 % in the green region as compared with those of blank PMMA (Fig. 5j), further indicating the potential of PMMA-CDs film as an agricultural light-conversion film.

Finally, the thermal stability and photostability of RH-CDs, PVP-CDs, and PMMA-CDs were analyzed by direct heating at different temperatures and exposure to UV light for up to one month, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the intensity of the emission peak of PVP-CDs hardly changes from room temperature (19 °C) to 80 °C and exhibits a significant enhancement from 100 °C to 160 °C, indicating the excellent thermal stability of the as-prepared CDs. Besides, the photostability of CDs was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 6c and d. After irradiation with UV light for 7 d, the PL intensity of CDs in DCM solution remained above 90 % of the initial value (Fig. 6c). And the PL intensity of CDs doped in PMMA film still retained above 83 % of the initial intensity after UV radiation of one month (Fig. 6d), indicating that both the as-prepared RH-CDs and PMMA-CDs possess excellent photostability. These results further demonstrate that the prepared RH-CDs have practical application potential as a stable fluorescent material.

In summary, an efficient synthetic strategy was proposed for the synthesis of red-emissive CDs by a one-step solvothermal method. The obtained RH-CDs display one emission peak centered at 594 nm and a shoulder emission peak at 630 nm, with a broad FWHM of ∼100 nm and a QY of 34 %. Both the reaction time and the acetic acid catalyst were found to have a significant effect on the red emission of CDs. The detailed mechanism study indicates that the superior performance of RH-CDs may originate from the increase in the size, sp2 domain, and graphite N contents of the CDs. Moreover, red emission powder with a QY of 12 % was prepared by dispersing RH-CDs in the PVP matrix, which could be used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications. Red and blue LED supplementary lights suitable for plant growth were also fabricated by combining the red emission powder and a blue LED (430 nm) chip. Additionally, RH-CDs have good compatibility with resins and could be doped homogeneously in PMMA. The obtained PMMA-CDs film exhibits green-to-red spectral conversion performance with a QY of 23 %. We believe that the successful synthesis of RH-CDs would not only provide inspiration for the rational design of red CDs, but also broaden the potential application of CDs in plant growth.

Cuixia Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Sainan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mengdi Liu: Software. Junhui He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019QY(Y)0503, 2017YFA0207102), the National Natural Science Foundation of China, (91963104, 52303286), Joint R&D Laboratory for Functional Agriculture Films, and Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2024.119601.
